items.question: How much does a BMW engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: It depends entirely on the engine. A reconditioned N47D20C (the 320d engine) runs £3,200–£5,500 supply-only [BMW-QUOTE], while a reconditioned S63B44 (M5/M6/X5M/X6M) runs £9,000–£14,000 [BMW-QUOTE]. Fitted costs add 10–24 labour hours depending on the engine. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which BMW engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The B58B30 is the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset — just 540 enquiries in 2025, mostly due-diligence not failures [BMW-VERIFIED]. From the older era, the M57D30 turbodiesel (E46/E90 330d) has the same bulletproof reputation. Both share one thing: regular oil changes are non-negotiable.

items.question: What is the most common BMW engine failure?
items.answer: The N47 timing chain stretch is our #1 enquiry driver — 1,450 enquiries in 2025, with the F30 3 Series alone generating 380 [BMW-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle from the rear of the engine is the early warning; ignored, chain slip means a full engine replacement at £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE]. The N57 and N63 are the next most common failure points.

items.question: Is the BMW 3 Series reliable?
items.answer: It depends entirely on the generation. The N47 era (E90/pre-2016 F30) has a reputation for timing chain issues — but a 2016+ F30 LCI with the B47 diesel is genuinely reliable. The G20 with the B58 is the safest 3 Series ever made. Use our model pages to check your specific generation.

items.question: Which BMW engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine itself is solid; the rear-mounted tensioner design is the problem. The N63's hot-vee architecture is also a high-cost risk. A 2016+ B47 or any B58 has no such caveat.

items.question: How do I know if my BMW timing chain is worn?
items.answer: Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning, sometimes with a chain-noise change under load. On the N47 and N57, listen for a metallic rattle from the rear of the engine on startup. On the B58, coolant loss from the expansion tank is the early signal. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,200–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW engine?
items.answer: Depends entirely on the car's value. Against a £35,000 G20, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 14% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £4,500 E90, a £4,000 N47 recon is 89% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our diagnostic calculator for your car.

items.question: How long do BMW engines last?
items.answer: A well-maintained BMW diesel (M57, N57, B47, B57) can exceed 200,000 miles. The B58 petrol is also proving durable — with proper servicing, 150,000+ miles is realistic. The N47's chain is the limiting factor, not the engine itself. The M54 and M57 are the high-mileage champions.

items.question: Which BMW generation should I buy?
items.answer: 2016+ F30 LCI with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol. You get the facelift architecture, the revised chain hardware, and the electronics update — plus depreciation has already done most of its work. For petrol performance, a G20 M340i is the sweet spot. For classic appeal, a sorted E46 330i is the keep-forever BMW.

items.question: How do I find my BMW engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most N47/B47 units, and on the cylinder head on N52/B58 engines. Or tell us your model year and variant (320d, 330i, 340i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.
